title: Port of Ghent

infobox.value: * [Antwerp](/wiki/Port_of_Antwerp "Port of Antwerp") * [Bruges-Zeebrugge](/wiki/Port_of_Zeebrugge "Port of Zeebrugge") * Ghent * [Ostend](/wiki/Port_of_Ostend "Port of Ostend")

* [Brussels](/wiki/Port_of_Brussels "Port of Brussels") * [Charleroi](/w/index.php?title=Port_of_Charleroi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Port of Charleroi (page does not exist)") * [Genk](/w/index.php?title=Port_of_Genk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Port of Genk (page does not exist)") * [La Louvière](/w/index.php?title=Port_of_La_Louvi%C3%A8re&action=edit&redlink=1 "Port of La Louvière (page does not exist)") * [Liège](/wiki/Port_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Port of Liège") * [Namur](/w/index.php?title=Port_of_Namur&action=edit&redlink=1 "Port of Namur (page does not exist)")

is the third busiest [seaport](/wiki/Port "Port") in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), located in [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") in the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"). The first port of Ghent was situated at the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") and later on at the [Leie](/wiki/Lys_(river) "Lys (river)"). Since the Middle Ages Ghent has sought for a connection to the sea. In the 13th century via the [Lieve canal](/w/index.php?title=Lieve_canal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lieve canal (page does not exist)") to the [Zwin](/wiki/Zwin "Zwin") near [Damme](/wiki/Damme "Damme"), in the 16th century via the [Sassevaart](/w/index.php?title=Sassevaart&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sassevaart (page does not exist)"), in the 17th century via the [Ghent–Bruges canal](/w/index.php?title=Ghent%E2%80%93Bruges_canal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ghent–Bruges canal (page does not exist)"). Since the 19th century, the [Ghent–Terneuzen Canal](/wiki/Ghent%E2%80%93Terneuzen_Canal "Ghent–Terneuzen Canal") connects the port via the [Western Scheldt](/wiki/Western_Scheldt "Western Scheldt") to the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The port of Ghent is accessible by ships of the [Panamax](/wiki/Panamax "Panamax") size, and in February 2015 the construction of a new lock at [Terneuzen](/wiki/Terneuzen "Terneuzen") was announced, which will maintain near-parity with those of the [Panama Canal expansion project](/wiki/Panama_Canal_expansion_project "Panama Canal expansion project").[[4]](#cite_note-IHSmaritime_2015-02-6-4)

["History of the port of Ghent"](https://web.archive.org/web/20040327040113/http://www.havengent.be/10_I_1OV.htm). Archived from [the original](http://www.havengent.be/10_i_1ov.htm#13) on 27 March 2004. Retrieved 3 March 2006.

The first port of Ghent was situated at the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") and later on at the [Leie](/wiki/Lys_(river) "Lys (river)"). Since the Middle Ages Ghent has sought for a connection to the sea. In the 13th century via the [Lieve canal](/w/index.php?title=Lieve_canal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lieve canal (page does not exist)") to the [Zwin](/wiki/Zwin "Zwin") near [Damme](/wiki/Damme "Damme"), in the 16th century via the [Sassevaart](/w/index.php?title=Sassevaart&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sassevaart (page does not exist)"), in the 17th century via the [Ghent–Bruges canal](/w/index.php?title=Ghent%E2%80%93Bruges_canal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ghent–Bruges canal (page does not exist)"). The **Port of Ghent** is the third busiest [seaport](/wiki/Port "Port") in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), located in [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") in the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"). The first port of Ghent was situated at the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") and later on at the [Leie](/wiki/Lys_(river) "Lys (river)"). Since the Middle Ages Ghent has sought for a connection to the sea. In the 13th century via the [Lieve canal](/w/index.php?title=Lieve_canal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lieve canal (page does not exist)") to the [Zwin](/wiki/Zwin "Zwin") near [Damme](/wiki/Damme "Damme"), in the 16th century via the [Sassevaart](/w/index.php?title=Sassevaart&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sassevaart (page does not exist)"), in the 17th century via the [Ghent–Bruges canal](/w/index.php?title=Ghent%E2%80%93Bruges_canal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ghent–Bruges canal (page does not exist)"). Since the 19th century, the [Ghent–Terneuzen Canal](/wiki/Ghent%E2%80%93Terneuzen_Canal "Ghent–Terneuzen Canal") connects the port via the [Western Scheldt](/wiki/Western_Scheldt "Western Scheldt") to the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The port of Ghent is accessible by ships of the [Panamax](/wiki/Panamax "Panamax") size, and in February 2015 the construction of a new lock at [Terneuzen](/wiki/Terneuzen "Terneuzen") was announced, which will maintain near-parity with those of the [Panama Canal expansion project](/wiki/Panama_Canal_expansion_project "Panama Canal expansion project").[[4]](#cite_note-IHSmaritime_2015-02-6-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Port_of_Ghent_aerial.jpg/250px-Port_of_Ghent_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Port_of_Ghent_aerial.jpg)

Aerial view of the Port of Ghent from the west

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Ghent%E2%80%93Terneuzen_Canal.jpg/250px-Ghent%E2%80%93Terneuzen_Canal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghent%E2%80%93Terneuzen_Canal.jpg)

[Ghent–Terneuzen Canal](/wiki/Ghent%E2%80%93Terneuzen_Canal "Ghent–Terneuzen Canal"), southwards view from a ship near [Arcelor Gent](/wiki/Arcelor "Arcelor")

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Port_of_Ghent&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 1251, the Lieve Canal was constructed in order to attempt to connect [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") to [Damme](/wiki/Damme "Damme"), which was at that time was situated at the [Zwin](/wiki/Zwin "Zwin"). However, the Zwin sanded up and the Lieve canal lost all relevance by the end of the fifteenth century.
In 1547, a second attempt was made by digging the Sassevaart (Sasse Canal), which became a busy trade route. In the sixteenth century however, the [European wars of religion](/wiki/European_wars_of_religion "European wars of religion") meant the end of navigation on the Sassevaart. This due to a traffic lock on both Western Scheldt and all other connecting waterways set by the Dutch. In the 17th century, a new attempt was made with the Ghent–[Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges")–[Ostend](/wiki/Ostend "Ostend") canal. But as a consequence of the abolition of the former trade privileges there was but little activity. The present canal (the [Ghent–Terneuzen Canal](/wiki/Ghent%E2%80%93Terneuzen_Canal "Ghent–Terneuzen Canal")) was finally dug under the rule of [William I](/wiki/William_I_of_the_Netherlands "William I of the Netherlands"), King of the Netherlands. In 1822 the final plan was ratified and in 1827 the works could be started. As waterborne traffic and port activities increased, the sea canal was extended several times:

* 1880–1881: digging of the *Voorhaven* and *Houtdok*
* 1900–1930: digging of the *Grootdok* with the *Noord-*, *Midden-* and *Zuiddok*
* 1931: digging of the *Schepen Sifferdok*

The works on the *Sifferdok* were complicated by the crisis of the thirties and by World Wars [I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [II](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). A revival was practically impossible as the dimensions of the lock were insufficient for the modern post-war seagoing vessels. In 1960 an agreement was signed between Belgium and the Netherlands, which stipulated that a new sealock was to be built and that the canal was to be adapted for vessels up to 80,000 tonnes [deadweight](/wiki/Deadweight_tonnage "Deadweight tonnage") (DWT). now one post-panamax vessel has already managed to pass through the existing lock.her name was alam permai with a deadweight of 87000 tonnes

* 1961–1968: lengthening of the *Sifferdok*
* 1966–1968: digging of the *Petroleumdok*
* 1968: inauguration of the new sealock
* 1970–1971/1975–1978: digging of the *[Rodenhuizedok](/w/index.php?title=Rodenhuizedok&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rodenhuizedok (page does not exist)")*
* 1996–today: digging of the *[Kluizendok](/w/index.php?title=Kluizendok&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kluizendok (page does not exist)")*

With the digging of the newest dock, the *[Kluizendok](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kluizendok "nl:Kluizendok")*, a new era dawned for Ghent. The first phase of the works was scheduled for completion in the autumn of 1999. From that moment onwards the first 1,200m of quay wall and waterfront sites behind became available. In 2005 it the *Kluizendok* became partially operational. It includes 100 [hectares](/wiki/Hectares "Hectares") directly fronting the dockside, suitable for logistics and industrial developments, and a further 100 hectares suitable for manufacturing and assembly industries without a direct interface to maritime transport.

In February 2015, Flanders and the Netherlands signed a treaty for the construction of a new lock at Terneuzen, scheduled for completion in 2021 and costing €920M.[[4]](#cite_note-IHSmaritime_2015-02-6-4) The new lock is about the same size as those of the contemporaneous [expansion project](/wiki/Panama_Canal_expansion_project "Panama Canal expansion project") of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal").[[4]](#cite_note-IHSmaritime_2015-02-6-4) In 2025, the [MV Astoria](/wiki/MS_Stockholm_(1946) "MS Stockholm (1946)") and the [MV Solong](/wiki/MV_Solong "MV Solong") arrived at the port of Ghent for scrapping.
